coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of magnetic carbon dots (CDs)-based hybrid material as an adsorbent for the removal of organic dye from water
coredata.dc:creator: Zourou, Adamantia
Ntziouni, Afroditi
Roman, Tiberiu
Tampaxis, Christos
Steriotis, Theodore
Gkouzia, Georgia
Alff, Lambert
Sanchez, David Emanuel
Terrones, Mauricio
Kordatos, Konstantinos Vassilios
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the rapid advancement of materials science and the desire for new functions has led to an enormous demand for novel materials, which could be used in various applications. As a result, hybrid materials have gained the interest of the scientific community due to the infinite combinations of individual components, which could lead to materials with unique properties. In this study, a carbon dots (CDs)-CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was successfully prepared through a solvothermal process and utilized as an adsorbent for the removal of Congo Red (CR) dye from aqueous environment. The nanohybrid material combines interactive surface functional groups due to CDs and high magnetic saturation due to CuFe2O4 nanoparticles, resulting in enhanced selectivity towards CR dye and easy separation after utilization with an external magnet. The as-prepared material was characterized by various techniques, including XRD, micro-Raman, FT-IR, HR-TEM/EDS, N2 porosimetry, and SQUID. Finally, its capability in the removal efficiency of CR dye was investigated at different initial CR concentrations, contact times and pH values via UV–Vis spectroscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper ferrite
Nanohybrid
Adsorption
Water treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of CR initial concentration, contact time and pH
Proposed adsorption mechanism of CR dye by N-CDs/CuFe2O4

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Adamantia Zourou would like to thank Special Account for Research Funding (E.L.K.E.) of National Technical University of Athens (N.T.U.A.) of Greece for funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of (a) CuFe2O4 and (b) N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Micro-Raman spectra of (a) CuFe2O4 and (b) N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of (a) CuFe2O4 and (b) N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HR-TEM (a–c) and HR-TEM/EDS (d–g) images of CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HR-TEM (a–c) and HR-TEM/EDS (d–i) images of N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) Pore size distribution of CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
M − H measurements of CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Adsorption capacity of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 at different CR concentrations and contact times (under natural pH = 5.5). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Structure of CR at pH > 5 and pH < 5, (b) Image of CR solution at pH = 3, 5.5 and 8, respectively. (c) UV–Vis spectrum of CR dye at pH = 3, pH = 5.5 (natural) and pH = 8. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Efficiency of the adsorption process under different pH values for initial CR concentration of (a) 5 mg/L, (b)10 mg/L, (c) 20 mg/L, (d) 30 mg/L and (e) 40 mg/L. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Langmuir isotherm (a) and Freundlich isotherm (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Pseudo-second order kinetic plot of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 at various initial concentrations of CR (5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L and 40 mg/L). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Recycling of the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 in the removal of CR dye. The initial concentration of CR was fixed at 5 mg/L and the contact time at 60 min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Calculated Langmuir and Freundlich models parameters.
Adsorption parameters from PSO kinetic equation for the adsorption of CR on N-CDs/CuFe2O4.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of magnetic carbon dots (CDs)-based hybrid material as an adsorbent for the removal of organic dye from water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the rapid advancement of materials science and the desire for new functions has led to an enormous demand for novel materials, which could be used in various applications. As a result, hybrid materials have gained the interest of the scientific community due to the infinite combinations of individual components, which could lead to materials with unique properties. In this study, a carbon dots (CDs)-CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was successfully prepared through a solvothermal process and utilized as an adsorbent for the removal of Congo Red (CR) dye from aqueous environment. The nanohybrid material combines interactive surface functional groups due to CDs and high magnetic saturation due to CuFe2O4 nanoparticles, resulting in enhanced selectivity towards CR dye and easy separation after utilization with an external magnet. The as-prepared material was characterized by various techniques, including XRD, micro-Raman, FT-IR, HR-TEM/EDS, N2 porosimetry, and SQUID. Finally, its capability in the removal efficiency of CR dye was investigated at different initial CR concentrations, contact times and pH values via UV–Vis spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, water quality encounters severe challenges due to the highly increasing population, industrialization and agricultural activities [1]. Unfortunately, it is estimated that 80 % of the global industrial and municipal water is released into the environment without undergoing any treatment; the percentage is even higher in the least developed countries due to the absence of proper facilities [2]. The presence of emerging contaminants into the aqueous environment, such as organic dyes [3], heavy metals [4], pesticides [5], pharmaceuticals [6], and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [7], is a major concern for humans, aquatic species, animals, and vegetations. Thus, their removal from aqueous environment is vital for the protection of both human health and ecosystems. The scientific community has dedicated efforts to the successful removal of water pollutants through various methods, including adsorption [8], photocatalysis [9], advanced oxidation processes [10], membrane separation [11], etc. Among these techniques, adsorption is the most popular because of its simplicity, high efficiency, low-cost, minimum energy requirement and absence of secondary waste production during the process [12].

Over the years, various materials have been utilized as adsorbents of water pollutants, including activated carbon, chitosan, natural and synthetic zeolites, etc. [13]. These days, the rapid advancement of materials science and the desire for new functions has led to an enormous demand for novel materials. As a result, the interest of scientific community has been shifted toward hybrid materials. The advent of hybrid materials has brought innovation into several fields, since the limitless combinations of individual components could lead to materials with superior properties [14]. In this broad array, one of the most significant classifications comprises hybrid materials based on carbon nanomaterials (e.g. fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, etc.) [15].

In this study, a carbon dots (CDs)-based hybrid material was developed for adsorption application. To begin with, CDs are the newest member of carbon-based nanomaterials family, discovered by Xu et al. [16] in 2004. More specifically, they are small (<10 nm) quasi-spherical nanoparticles which consist of either an amorphous or a crystalline carbon core surrounded by various surface functionalities (e.g. –OH, –COOH, –CO, –C–O–C, etc.) [17]. Recently, CDs have received much attention in environmental applications due to their unique characteristics, including fluorescence, abundance of surface functional groups, ease-of-preparation, and low or even non-toxicity. They have been mostly employed for the detection of various water pollutants, such as radioactive U(VI) [18], heavy metal ions Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ Co2+ [19], Cd2+ [20], etc. In addition, CDs have been utilized in combination with photocatalytic materials (e.g. TiO2, g-C3N4) for the efficient degradation of organic dyes. Based on the results, the photodegradation capability was significantly enhanced when CDs were integrated [21,22]. CDs have been also employed as adsorbents [23] of water contaminants due to the presence of abundant surface functional groups, as well as the ease of surface functionalization, which could be tailored to enhance selectivity towards specific pollutants. However, due to their high hydrophilicity, they cannot be easily separated from the solution after their utilization. Therefore, their combination with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) has been considered as an ideal option, as they could be easily retrieved by applying an external magnetic field [24,25]. Spinel ferrites (SFs) with the general formula MFe2O4 (where M: divalent metallic cation) represent an important class of magnetic materials. The cubic cell of MFe2O4 consists of 56 atoms: 32 closely packed oxygen anions and 24 M2+ and Fe3+ cations occupying 8 tetrahedral sites and 16 octahedral sites, respectively [26]. SFs have gained much interest as adsorbents of water pollutants because of their high surface area, low-cost preparation and magnetic properties [27]. Consequently, the development of a CDs-SFs hybrid adsorbent combines not only a large number of active sites which could improve the interactions of the adsorbent with various water pollutants, but also effective segregation after use due to the magnetic properties [28–31].

Herein, a N-doped CDs (N-CDs)/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was synthesized via a facile solvothermal process and characterized by several techniques, such as X-ray Diffraction (XRD) Analysis, micro-Raman Spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscopy, High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) mapping and N2 porosimetry. Furthermore, the magnetic behavior of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 at room temperature was studied using a Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID) Magnetometer. The as-prepared N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was utilized as an adsorbent of Congo Red (CR) dye. CR is the sodium salt of 3,3′-([1,1′-biphenyl]-4,4′-diyl)bis (4-aminonaphthalene-1-sulfonic acid) and it is one of the most common synthetic azo dyes used in the textile industry, presenting high toxicity to several organisms, while it is a suspected carcinogen and mutagen agent [25,32]. Thus, its removal from aqueous environment has been of fundamental importance for the protection of both human health and nature. In this work, batch experiments were carried out in order to investigate the effects of various parameters, such as initial CR dye concentration, contact time and pH on the adsorption performance of the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 via Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) Spectroscopy. Finally, isotherms and kinetic models were used to describe the behavior of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid adsorbent depending on the above parameters.

Citric acid (C6H8O7,
≥
99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O,
≥
98 %), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, ≥99 %), sodium acetate (NaAc, ≥99 %), as well as, polyethylene glycol (PEG, average Mn 4.000) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany). Copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O,
≥
99 %) and ethylene glycol (EG,
≥
99 %) were purchased from Chem-Lab NV (Chem-Lab NV, Belgium) and Fisher Scientific (Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively.

XRD Analysis was carried out on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (Bruker, USA). Micro-Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope equipped with a 532 nm laser (Renishaw, UK). FT-IR spectra were obtained with a Jasco FT-IR 4200 spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and resolution of 4 cm−1, using KBr pellets (Jasco, Japan). HR-TEM was conducted on a Technai G2 20 X-TWIN TEM (FEI, USA) equipped with EDS. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was estimated by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms using an Autosorb-ASiQ porosimeter (Quantachrome, USA) at 77 K. Prior to measurements, the samples were degassed at 10−6 mbar and 80 °C for 12 h. The magnetic properties were studied by Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID) Magnetometer, using the Magnetic Properties Measurement System (MPMS) by Quantum Design. The measurements were performed at 300 K with external magnetic fields up to 4 kOe. Finally, UV–Vis measurements were performed on a Varian Cary 50 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA).

N-CDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method, using citric acid as carbon source and urea as both carbon and nitrogen source. More specifically, 3.00 g citric acid and 3.00 g urea were dissolved into 10.0 mL of DI water, under vigorous magnetic stirring for 15 min. The colorless aqueous solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting aqueous dispersion, with a dark brown color, was centrifuged at 6.000 rpm for 30 min to remove by-products. The supernatant liquid was collected and filtered with a 0.22 μm PES syringe filter, to eliminate aggregates. Finally, the filtered N-CDs were further purified using a dialysis membrane of 8–10 kDa. The as-prepared N-CDs solution was stored for further use.

The Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was prepared via a facile solvothermal method. To begin with, 0.50 mL of the N-CDs solution, prepared as it was mentioned above, were evaporated under reduced pressure, using a rotary evaporator, in order to remove the aqueous solvent. Subsequently, the precipitate was dispersed into 40.0 mL of EG by ultra-sonication for 10 min. Following this, CuCl2∙2H2O and FeCl3∙6H2O were added into the above dispersion, so that the molar ratio of Cu:Fe was 1:2. At this point, 3.60 g NaAc and 1.00 g PEG were added, followed by magnetic stirring for 30 min. Following this, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was complete, the mixture was centrifuged at 6.000 rpm for 30 min and the precipitate was washed with excess of DI water and acetone. Finally, the resulting material was dried overnight at 60 °C in air and the Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid adsorbent was obtained in a powder form. For comparison reasons, CuFe2O4 was similarly synthesized in the absence of N-CDs.

Batch adsorption experiments were performed to evaluate the behavior of the Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent in the removal of CR dye from aqueous environment, by varying the initial CR concentration, contact time and pH value.

In order to calibrate the UV–Vis equipment, an initial stock solution of CR (50 mg/L) was prepared. Following this, the calibration curve of CR included aqueous solutions with concentration ranging from 0.1 mg/L to 50 mg/L was determined at λ max = 498 nm. The procedure was repeated for pH = 3 and pH = 8 and the CR calibration curves were measured at λ max = 578 nm and λ max = 498 nm, respectively. The pH of all the CR initial solutions was adjusted at 3 or 8 with the addition of HCl or NaOH aqueous solutions, respectively. All the CR calibration curves are presented in Fig. S1.

Five different initial CR aqueous solutions of 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L and 40 mg/L were prepared as starting solutions. Subsequently, 10.0 mg of the Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent were added into 5.0 mL of each initial CR solution and monitored over time (15, 30, 60, 120, 180 and 240 min), followed by magnetic separation with an external magnet. The remaining CR dye in the aqueous solutions was estimated via UV–Vis spectroscopy. In addition, the effect of the initial pH on the removal efficiency of CR was assessed. Therefore, 10.0 mg of Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 were added into 5.0 mL of each initial CR solution, with pH = 3, 5.5 (natural) and 8. After 60 min, the adsorbent was removed using an external magnet and the remaining CR dye was determined by UV–Vis spectroscopy.

Finally, the amount of the adsorbed CR (mg/g) was calculated based on the mass balance equation given in Equation (1) [33].
(1)
q e
=
(
C
0
−
C e
)
× V
W
where qe (mg/g): the equilibrium adsorption capacity per gram of dry adsorbent, Co (mg/L): the initial concentration of CR dye in the solution, Ce (mg/L): the equilibrium concentration of CR dye in the solution, V (L): the volume of the solution, and W (g): the weight of the adsorbent.

In this study, Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was synthesized via an electrostatic self-assembly method using a solvothermal process. The as-prepared Ν-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was characterized by XRD, micro-Raman, FT-IR, HR-TEM/EDS and N2 adsorption/desorption volumetric analysis. Furthermore, the magnetic behavior of the adsorbent was studied by SQUID Magnetometer. Finally, its adsorption performance in the removal of CR organic dye from aqueous solutions was evaluated at different concentrations, contact times and pH values, via UV–Vis spectroscopy.

The crystallographic structure of CuFe2O4 nanoparticles and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was studied by powder XRD analysis. In Fig. 1 the XRD patterns of both CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 are illustrated. As it is observed in Fig. 1 (a), the diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 30.15ο, 35.51ο, 43.30ο, 50.43ο, 56.96ο, 62.62ο and 74.14° are assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) crystal planes of CuFe2O4 with cubic spinel structure, respectively [24,25]. Similarly, in the XRD pattern of the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material (Fig. 1 (b)), the observed diffraction peaks correspond to the crystal planes of CuFe2O4, as it was mentioned above. Noticeably, no diffraction peaks that are correlated with the N-CDs are observed. This is an issue that has been addressed by several scientific studies [34–36]. The fact that the diffraction peak of CDs does not appear in the XRD spectrum of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 may be attributed to their extremely small size or/and their homogeneous distribution on CuFe2O4 surface. Therefore, further characterization techniques are necessary in order to confirm their presence in the nanohybrid material.

It is well known that micro-Raman spectroscopy is one of the most commonly used techniques for the characterization of carbon-based nanomaterials. In Fig. 2 the micro-Raman spectra of both CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 are shown. More specifically, in Fig. 2 (a), the observed peaks at 215 cm−1, 278 cm−1, 496 cm−1 and 681 cm−1 correspond to the F2g (1), Eg, F2g (2) and A1g modes of phonon vibrations in the CuFe2O4 nanoparticles, respectively [37]. There is also a band at 389 cm−1 which indicates the presence of Fe2O3 [24]. In Fig. 2 (b), there are two peaks located at 1358 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1, which are assigned to the D- and G-band, respectively. The G-band is characteristic for the sp2 hybridized carbon nanomaterials, while the D-band corresponds to the structural defects and disorders of the carbon lattice. Moreover, the peaks located bellow 1000 cm−1 are assigned to CuFe2O4. Herein, the presence of both D- and G-bands in the micro-Raman spectrum of the sample strongly indicates the successful combination of N-CDs with CuFe2O4 nanoparticles. In addition, the intensity ratio of D to G band (ID/IG), is used as an estimating measure for the structural defects of the graphitic domains. Herein, the high ID/IG ratio demonstrates that N-CDs have high content of sp3 carbon atoms [15]. Furthermore, a broad band observed at approximately 2700 cm−1 corresponds to the 2D band, which indicates the presence of few-layered graphitic domains.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed for the identification of functional groups present in CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 samples, as it is observed in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectrum of CuFe2O4 which is indicated in Fig. 3 (a) presents two absorption peaks at 593 cm−1 and 427 cm−1, which are assigned to the characteristic Fe–O and Cu–O stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption peaks located at 3433 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H stretching and bending vibrations of both remaining water molecules, and metal oxides hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1384 cm−1 is also assigned to the presence of metal oxides hydroxyl groups [38]. Furthermore, the peaks located at 2918 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–H stretching vibrations of CH2 and CH3 [24,25]. The presence of these groups may be attributed to the organic precursors used for the solvothermal synthesis of CuFe2O4 (e.g. PEG, NaAc, EG). In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 presented in Fig. 3 (b), the above absorption peaks could be also observed. However, a new absorption peak can be noticed at 1575 cm−1, which is related to the stretching vibrations of CC, indicating the successful synthesis of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 [39]. It is important to note that the absorption peak at 1575 cm−1 is also present in the FT-IR of N-CDs which is illustrated in Fig. S2.

The morphology of CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 was studied via HR-TEM analysis, followed by elemental mapping via EDS, as it is presented in Figs. 4 and 5
, respectively. As it is observed in Fig. 4(a–c), CuFe2O4 nanoparticles with spherical-ellipsoidal shape and an average size of ∼ 5 nm have been successfully formed via the electrostatic self-assembly method. Agglomeration phenomena are also observed due to the powerful inherent magnetic interactions of the nanoparticles. On the other hand, N-CDs/CuFe2O4 presents different morphology compared to CuFe2O4. As it can be clearly seen in Fig. 5(a–c), the nanohybrid material possesses core-shell structural features, where N-CDs layer is assembled onto the CuFe2O4 core. The core-shell material presents an average size of ∼200 nm. Based on EDS analysis, the presence of Cu, Fe and O is revealed in both samples originating from CuFe2O4. However, two new elements, C and N, are observed in the HR-TEM/EDS of the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 (Fig. 5(d–i)) indicating the successful incorporation of the N-CDs onto the CuFe2O4 surface.

The textural properties of CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 were evaluated via N2 adsorption/desorption volumetric analysis and the results are illustrated in Fig. 6 . According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of both materials exhibit Type IV isotherm, with type H4 hysteresis loop, indicating the coexistence of both mesopores (2–50 nm) and micropores (<2 nm) [40]. Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) analysis of the isotherms, based on silica kernel and cylindrical pore geometry revealed pore diameters of approximately 4 nm, followed by a very wide distribution of larger sizes reaching into the macropore regime. In addition, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller Area (ABET) of CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 was estimated at 90 m2/g (t-method micropore area = 23 m2/g) and 35 m2/g (t-method micropore area = 9 m2/g), respectively. The decrease of ABET observed in the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material (around 40 %) is coupled with a similar reduction of the micropore area and could be attributed to partial blockage of the pore network of CuFe2O4 by N-CDs.

The magnetic properties of both CuFe2O4 and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 samples were measured by MPMS SQUID magnetometer under applied field of 40 kOe at 300 K. The obtained M − H curves are presented in Fig. 7 , where the soft ferromagnetic nature of the samples is revealed. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of CuFe2O4 was found to be 53.54 emu/g, while the Ms of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 is reduced to 44.98 emu/g. The reduction is attributed to the presence of N-CDs, which are inherently non-magnetic. Although, the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material continues to exhibit commendable magnetic properties, thereby establishing its suitability for application as a magnetic adsorbent [41–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CR initial concentration, contact time and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The initial organic dye concentration, the contact time, as well as the pH are among the most important parameters in the removal efficiency of the adsorbent. In order to investigate the effect of both initial CR concentration and contact time on the adsorption efficiency of N-CDs/CuFe2O4, five CR initial solutions of 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L and 40 mg/L were selected, while the contact time was varied from 15 to 240 min. The corresponding data are shown in Fig. 8 , where it can be clearly observed that the variation of the initial CR concentration from 5 mg/L to 40 mg/L influences the adsorption capacity, by increasing it from 2.51 mg/g to 18.40 mg/g, respectively. Furthermore, the adsorption capacity is dramatically increased at the initial stage and then tends to slow down near the equilibrium. This may be attributed to the abundance of vacant surface sites available on N-CDs/CuFe2O4 at the beginning. The number of vacant surface sites is steadily decreased with the prolongation of contact time and the residual vacant surface sites cannot be easily occupied due to the repulsive forces among CR adsorbed on N-CDs/CuFe2O4 and solution phase [24,25,33].

The effect of the initial pH on the CR adsorption by N-CDs/CuFe2O4 was investigated at different pH values (pH = 3, pH = 5.5, pH = 8) with a contact time of 60 min. Herein, it is important to note that CR is a dipolar molecule. At pH
≥
5, the unprotonated anionic form is present, while at pH < 5 the protonated CR consists of two tautomeric and one azoic form, as it is shown in Fig. 9 (a) [44]. Depending on pH value, the color of CR dye changes from red for pH > 5 to blue for pH < 5 (Fig. 9 (b)). The UV–Vis spectra of CR solution at different pH values are presented in Fig. 9 (c). The main absorption peak noticed at 498 nm for pH
>
5 is ascribed to the π-π∗ transitions of the azo group (
−
NN
−
) [45]. However, when the pH value is decreased, the above peak is shifted to higher wavelengths up to 655 nm for pH = 3, while the intensity is also decreased. This may be attributed to the partially self-association of CR monomers as anionic dimers in face-to-face arrangement to minimize their hydrophobic interaction with water [46]. In addition, the peak at approximately 340 nm is assigned to the presence of aromatic rings in the molecule structure.

In Fig. 10
the results associated with the efficiency of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 in the removal of CR aqueous solutions (5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L and 40 mg/L), under different pH values (pH = 3, pH = 5.5, pH = 8) at a contact time of 60 min are presented. The efficiency of CR adsorption process is greatly influenced by the pH value. As it is observed, the adsorption capacity is highly increased at the acidic environment, where the CR molecule exists in a cationic form, while the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. Consequently, the electrostatic interactions between the adsorbent and the adsorbate are enhanced. On the other hand, at basic pH the anionic form of CR prevails resulting in inter ionic repulsion between similar charge [47]. For comparison purposes, the performance of CuFe2O4 NPs in the CR removal was also investigated and the results are presented in Fig. S3, as well as, Table S1.

Adsorption isotherms modeling is fundamental in order to study the interactions occurred in an adsorbent-adsorbate system. Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms were used to describe the interactions between CR dye and N-CDs/CuFe2O4. The former assumes that the adsorption process can only occur homogeneously at the surface monolayer and the latter describes the multilayer adsorption of adsorbate on a heterogeneous adsorbent surface.

The Langmuir isotherm model is represented by Equation (3) [33],
(3)
1
q e
=
1
q max
+
1
K L
q max
1
C e
where qe is the amount of adsorbed CR on the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 surface at equilibrium (mg/g), qmax is the maximum amount of adsorption with complete monolayer coverage on N-CDs/CuFe2O4 surface (mg/g), KL is the Langmuir constant (L/mg), and Ce is the amount of CR remaining in the initial solution after the adsorption process (mg/L).

The Freundlich isotherm model is represented by Equation (4) [33],
(4)
log
q e
= log
K F
+
1 n
log
C e
where KF and n are Freundlich adsorption isotherms constants indicating the linearity/nonlinearity between solution concentration and adsorption rates of the materials. The values of KF and n can be calculated by plotting log Ce against log qe.

The plots of experimental data based on Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms are presented in Fig. 11. Additionally, the calculated isotherm parameters of both models are shown in Table 1 . The higher correlation coefficient (r2) of the linearized form of Langmuir (0.99299), compared to that of Freundlich (0.97760) suggests that the Langmuir model is more suitable to represent the adsorption of CR by N-CDs/CuFe2O4. Hence, CR dye is homogeneously adsorbed on the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 surface. Nevertheless, the Freundlich model also gave a significantly high correlation coefficient value. The above results indicate that CR adsorption includes both physical and chemical mechanisms. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that the n value obtained by Freundlich isotherm is larger than unity, which shows strong interaction forces between CR dye and N-CDs/CuFe2O4 [33,48].

Kinetic study is an important factor in order to understand the mechanism of CR dye adsorption onto N-CDs/CuFe2O4. The pseudo-second-order (PSO) model, which is based on the assumption that chemisorption is the rate determining step, was used for kinetic data analysis. The linear form of the PSO model is described by Equation (5) [33],
(5)
1
q t
=
1
K 2
q
2 e
2
+
1
q
2 e
t
where qt is the amount of the adsorbed CR at time t (mg/g), t is the time (h), K2 is the PSO constant (g/mg/h) and q2e is the amount of adsorbed CR at equilibrium for a PSO kinetic. K2 and q2e can be calculated by linear plot of t/qt against t.

Recyclability is an important strategy to demonstrate the potential effect of sustainable adsorbents on environmental remediation [49]. Therefore, every adsorbent should be recycled without undergoing a significant reduction in its removal efficiency. Herein, the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent was utilized for 5 cycles and the results are presented in Fig. 13 . Even after the last cycle, the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent demonstrated very good reusability, maintaining a removal efficiency of over 85 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed adsorption mechanism of CR dye by N-CDs/CuFe2O4
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption mechanism depends on several interactions, which may be occurred between the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent and the CR dye. More specifically, the adsorption of CR molecules onto N-CDs/CuFe2O4 could be attributed to: (i) π-π interactions formed between the N-CDs basal planes and the aromatic structure (benzene/naphthalene ring) of CR, (ii) hydrogen bonding between the
−
OH/
−
COOH groups of N-CDs or/and CuFe2O4 and the –NH2 groups of CR, (iii) hydrogen bonding between the
−
OH/
−
COOH groups of N-CDs or/and CuFe2O4 and the negatively charged oxygen atoms of CR, (iv) electrostatic interactions between the opposite electric charges of CR dye and N-CDs/CuFe2O4; herein, it is important to note that the pH value plays a significant role, since at acidic environment the CR molecule exists in a cationic form resulting in enhanced interactions with the oxygen-containing functional groups of the N-CDs/CuFe2O4 adsorbent.

In this study, N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was successfully synthesized through solvothermal method, and characterized via various techniques such as XRD, micro-Raman, FT-IR, HR-TEM/EDS, N2 Adsorption/Desorption and SQUID. The N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material was studied as an adsorbent for the removal of CR dye from aqueous solutions at different initial CR concentrations, contact times and pH values. After its utilization, the adsorbent could be easily separated from water with an external magnet. In addition, it presented very good reusability with a removal efficiency of over 85 % even after five cycles. Langmuir, as well as, Freundlich adsorption isotherms were used to describe the interactions between CR dye and N-CDs/CuFe2O4; both models presented high correlation coefficient values, indicating that physical and chemical interactions forces occurred. Furthermore, based on the kinetic studies, the adsorption process of N-CDs/CuFe2O4 was fitted well with PSO kinetic equation which indicates that chemisorption is the rate determining step. In conclusion, based on the adsorption data results, the simple solvothermal process and the ease of magnetic separation, N-CDs/CuFe2O4 nanohybrid material is considered a promising adsorbent for the removal of CR dye from water environment.

Adamantia Zourou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Afroditi Ntziouni: Formal analysis, Data curation. Tiberiu Roman: Formal analysis, Data curation. Christos Tampaxis: Formal analysis, Data curation. Theodore Steriotis: Writing – review & editing. Georgia Gkouzia: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lambert Alff: Writing – review & editing. David Emanuel Sanchez: Formal analysis, Data curation. Mauricio Terrones: Writing – review & editing. Konstantinos Vasileiou Kordatos: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Adamantia Zourou would like to thank Special Account for Research Funding (E.L.K.E.) of National Technical University of Athens (N.T.U.A.) of Greece for funding.
